items.correct: The small projecting line at the end of letter strokes.
items.misconception: A whole typeface that looks formal.
items.why: Students conflate the FEATURE (the small foot/stroke) with the CLASSIFICATION (serif typefaces, which have them). Sans serif means 'without serifs' - those little projections at letter endings.

items.q: What did Pollock mean by his drip paintings being 'in the painting'?
items.correct: He worked from all sides, making the act of painting central to the meaning.
items.misconception: He literally fell into wet paint.
items.why: Students take 'in the painting' literally. Pollock meant his BODILY ENGAGEMENT - working around and over the canvas on the floor - was integral to the work, not just the marks left behind.

items.q: Why is understanding patronage important for studying historical art?
items.correct: Patrons commissioned art for specific purposes, shaping content and form.
items.misconception: Artists worked freely - patronage was irrelevant to the artwork.
items.why: Students romanticise the lone artist. Most pre-modern art was COMMISSIONED, with patrons specifying subject, materials, even composition. Understanding the commission illuminates choices the artist made.

items.q: Which designer is famed for using negative space in mid-twentieth-century film posters?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Saul Bass. “Paul Rand” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which colour model is standard for full-colour print production?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: CMYK. “RGB” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many ink colours does CMYK printing use?
items.why: Exact answer: 4 colours. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
